coredata.dc:description: The high-efficiency removal and real-time monitoring of chromate (Cr(VI)) in wastewater remains an enormous challenge, and few studies considered the use of nanofiltration (NF) membranes for these purposes. Herein, Ni2+-doped carbon dots (Ni-CDs) with a highly negative charge, photosensitivity and acid resistance were successfully coupled onto a polyethersulfone substrate via interfacial polymerization (IP) as an active layer (termed as x%-Ni-CDs-M, and the mass percentages were x = 0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, and 1.0). Ni-CDs-Ms not only naturally inherited the acid restitance and nanozyme activities, but also greatly increased the hydrophilicity and negative charge property of the membrane surface, resulting in membranes with excellent photodynamic anti-bacterial, acid resistant, and divalent anions separation abilities. Most significantly, both the Ni-CDs and 0.25–1.0%-Ni-CDs-Ms can selectively detect Cr(VI) in wastewater via an inner filter effect. In particular, the sensitivity of the Ni-CDs-Ms-based sensor was nearly 9 times greater than that of the Ni-CDs-based chemosensor. 0.75%-Ni-CDs-M was therefore successfully applied for the highly efficient removal and real-time monitoring of Cr(VI) in wastewater. The proposed strategy is dedicated to real-time warning of abnormal changes in Cr(VI) level in wastewater, and provides the technical support for the prevention and handling of sudden Cr(VI) incidents in the environment.

head.ce:title: Biofouling/acid resistant nanofiltration membranes interfacially polymerized with nickel-doped carbon dots for highly efficient removal and real-time monitoring of chromate in wastewater

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key laboratory of Functional Molecule Design and Interfacial Process and Key Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control and Resource Reuse, Anhui Jianzhu University, Anhui, Hefei, 230022, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Shuai Dai and Pingping Wu equally contributed.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The high-efficiency removal and real-time monitoring of chromate (Cr(VI)) in wastewater remains an enormous challenge, and few studies considered the use of nanofiltration (NF) membranes for these purposes. Herein, Ni2+-doped carbon dots (Ni-CDs) with a highly negative charge, photosensitivity and acid resistance were successfully coupled onto a polyethersulfone substrate via interfacial polymerization (IP) as an active layer (termed as x%-Ni-CDs-M, and the mass percentages were x = 0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, and 1.0). Ni-CDs-Ms not only naturally inherited the acid restitance and nanozyme activities, but also greatly increased the hydrophilicity and negative charge property of the membrane surface, resulting in membranes with excellent photodynamic anti-bacterial, acid resistant, and divalent anions separation abilities. Most significantly, both the Ni-CDs and 0.25–1.0%-Ni-CDs-Ms can selectively detect Cr(VI) in wastewater via an inner filter effect. In particular, the sensitivity of the Ni-CDs-Ms-based sensor was nearly 9 times greater than that of the Ni-CDs-based chemosensor. 0.75%-Ni-CDs-M was therefore successfully applied for the highly efficient removal and real-time monitoring of Cr(VI) in wastewater. The proposed strategy is dedicated to real-time warning of abnormal changes in Cr(VI) level in wastewater, and provides the technical support for the prevention and handling of sudden Cr(VI) incidents in the environment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: As an inhaled/ingested toxicant, hexavalent chromium Cr(VI) easily invades human tissue via the digestive system, respiratory tract, skin, and mucosa, resulting in an allergy upon exposure, and even inherited genetic defects [1–3]. Long-term exposure to a Cr(VI) atmosphere or direct inhalation poses a carcinogenic risk and can cause many health problems. Even short-term contact with Cr(VI) can have adverse effects at the contact site. The ingestion of large doses of Cr(VI) leads to kidney and liver damage, nausea, gastrohelcosis, spasm, and even death [4–6]. Furthermore, Cr(VI) to the environment can pose a persistent risk, and has a lethal effect on aquatic life. Cr(VI) pollution is closely related to human production and life [7,8]. Industrial activities involving chromium discharge millions of tons of wastewater containing Cr(VI) every year, and the Cr(VI) content in the wastewater can be as high as 40 mg/L [9]. Such a large amount of Cr(VI)-containing wastewater, if not effectively processed but recklessly discharged into natural waterways, will greatly endanger human health and environmental safety [5,6]. The vast majority of chromium minerals are nontoxic Cr(III) resources, which eventually evolve into Cr(VI) pollution due to the natural oxidization of Cr(III) under oxygen-rich conditions. Therefore, high levels of Cr(VI) can be detected in aquifers [10,11]. Given the high pollution and bioaccumulation of Cr(VI) [12], strict regulations have been formulated to normalize the discharge of Cr(VI)-containing wastewater and Cr(VI) content in drinking water. The Cr(VI) concentration in wastewater was strictly lower than 0.5 mg/L in China (GB8978-1996). The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA) has stipulated a total chromium concentration of 100 ppb as the maximum contaminant level in drinking water [13,14]. The World Health Organization (WHO) has declared that the maximum content of Cr(VI) in drinking water is 0.05 mg/L (WHO, 2017). As a result, new strategies for eliminating Cr(VI) in effluents are necessary to address global environmental concerns over carcinogenic Cr(VI) and satisfy the increasing demand for clean drinking water.

Many approaches, for instance, physical/chemical adsorption [15–21], ion exchange [22,23], chemical reduction [24–31], biological reduction [32–36], solvent extraction [37], dialysis [38,39], physical/chemical adsorption [40], have been developed for removing Cr(VI) or converting Cr(VI) into nontoxic forms, which greatly contributed to the clean water supply and water crisis alleviation worldwide [41–44]. Nevertheless, the inherent limitations of these approaches, such as slower kinetics for biological reduction [45], the risks of excess COD [46], impose restrictions on their wide application [47]. Reversely, membrane separation offers the advantages of continuous and stable long-time operation, cost-effectiveness, energy-efficiency, and eco-friendliness [48,49]. Among them, polyamide (PA) nanofiltration (NF) membranes are shaped sub-nanometer-level pores in the skin layer by interfacial polymerization (IP) [50]. These pores, which can be finely regulated by the integration of nanomaterials into the skin layer, enable the effective permeation of only those ions that have a diameter less than a certain value, endowing the NF membrane with the size sieving functionality [51]. Furthermore, the charged surfaces of the NF membrane show a high rejection efficiency toward di-/multivalent ions via the Donnan effect. Accordingly, under the synergistic action of the size sieving and the Donnan effect, monovalent ions can permeate through the membrane with a high flux, thus realizing the efficient separation of monovalent and divalent ions [52]. Nevertheless, until recently, few studies reported the use of NF membranes for the removal of Cr(VI) in effluents [53].

Conjugating charged carbon dots (CDs) into/onto the active layer of the NF membrane to regulate the pore size and surface charge seems to be a promising protocol for Cr(VI) removal. In this study, Ni2+-doped carbon dots (Ni-CDs) were synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal approach with Na2EDTA, piperidine (PIP), and NiCl2·H2O as precursors. Different amounts of Ni-CDs (0-1.0 wt% with an interval of 0.25), together with PIP and trimesoyl chloride (TMC), were chemically covered the polyethersulfone (PES) substrate membrane as an active layer via IP, and the resulting membranes were denoted as x%-Ni-CDs-M (x = 0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, and 1.0), and were collectively called Ni-CDs-Ms. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, zeta-potential analysis, X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), water contact angle measurement, atomic force microscopy (AFM), excitation and emission spectra, etc., were selectively utilized for the characterization of the Ni-CDs and PA membranes. The feasibility of sensing Cr(VI) in complex samples using both Ni-CDs and 0.75%-Ni-CDs-M as fluorescent probes was studied. PA membranes were applied to clean Cr(VI) in effluents, and the effects of the pH, Cr(VI) concentration, operation pressure/time, and acidification time on the removal efficiency were also considered. The mechanisms about the anti-biofouling/acid, and the elimination and real-time monitoring of Cr(VI) of the PA membrane were reasonably elucidated.

Ni-CDs were fabricated using Na2EDTA, PIP, and NiCl2·H2O as raw materials by a one-pot hydrothermal strategy. Briefly, PIP (0.32g), Na2EDTA (0.35g), and NiCl2·H2O (0.1g) were dissolved in 20 mL deionized water via ultrasonication for 10 min. The transparent mixture was decanted in a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. The naturally cooled product was preliminarily purified by centrifuging at 10,000 rpm for 5 min. The resulting supernatant was dialyzed via a dialysis bag for 48 h and then condensed to half its original volume. The concentrated solution was freeze-dried to obtain an ashen powder, which was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for subsequent use. The CDs were known as Ni-CDs due to the use of Ni ions as dopants. Ni-free CDs were also synthesized for comparison following the same process as Ni-CDs, which were referred to abbreviated as nNi-CDs.

NF membranes were prepared on PES substrate by IP. First, a certain amount of Ni-CDs (0, 0.25, 0.50, 0.75, and 1.00 wt%) was dispersed in an aqueous solution containing 1.00 wt % PIP by ultrasonication to form mixtures. Prior to IP, the PES substrate membranes were washed with deionized water several times, and then completely air-dried. The mixture containing Ni-CDs and PIP above was poured into the plastic frame. The PES membrane surface frame was soaked in the aqueous solution for 3 min to guarantee that the Ni-CDs and PIP adequately diffused into the porous support. Thereafter, the excess mixture was cleared away by the compressed air, and the PIP and Ni-CDs saturated membranes were subsequently immersed in an n-hexane solution containing 0.25 wt % TMC for 2 min. The IP reaction was broken by deionized water and the membranes were washed with n-hexane and deionized water several times. Finally, the membranes were dried in an oven at 60 °C for 8 min for further IP and n-hexane evaporation. The resulting TFN membranes were referred to as x%-Ni-CDs-M (x = 0, 0.25, 0.50, 0.75, and 1.00). These membranes were stored in ultrapure (UP) water at 10 °C for other purposes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Separation performance evaluation of NF membranes
ce:section.ce:para:
The separation performances of 0%-Ni-CDs-M, 0.25%-Ni-CDs-M, 0.5%-Ni-CDs-M, 0.75%-Ni-CDs-M, and 1.00%-Ni-CDs-M were tested in an ultrafiltration cup (Dead end device). The effective surface area of the NF membranes was tailored to be 12.56 cm2. Aqueous solutions containing 1000 ppm inorganic salts, including MgSO4, MgCl2, Na2SO4, and NaCl, and nutrient ions, such as HPO4 2−, SO4 2−, H2PO4 −, and NO3 −, were prepared as feed solutions to evaluate the separation performance of the NF membranes. The water flux (Jw) and salt rejection (Rs) were obtained by Eqs. (1) and (2), respectively,
(1)

where ΔV and Δt are the permeate volume change and filtration time, respectively. Cp and Cf are the salt concentrations in the feed and permeate solutions, respectively. The concentrations of inorganic salts and nutrient ions in the solutions were calculated by their electrical conductivities and ion chromatography, respectively. The operational pressure was totally set at 3 bar during the NF process unless specifically mentioned.

One piece of NF membrane was soaked in an aqueous solution with pH 3.0 for different durations at room temperature. The membrane was then taken out and washed with DDW several times to eliminate residual HCl. This process is termed acidification treatment. Moreover, a piece of identical NF membrane without any treatment served as a control. The changes in salt rejection and permeate flux of these two membranes were surveyed to validate the acid resistance of the NF membranes at 3 bar of operating pressure.

The retention rate of Cr(VI) was determined using the aforementioned ultrafiltration cup at pH 10 and 3.0 bar. Standard solutions of Cr(VI) were prepared using analytical purity grade K2CrO4 as a sample. A feed solution comprising 500 ppm Cr(VI) was performed. The Cr(VI) concentrations in the feed and permeation solutions were analyzed by UV–vis spectrophotometry and the rejection of Cr(VI) was calculated by Eq. (2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence-response of Ni-CDs and 0.75%-Ni-CDs-M to Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, Ni-CDs (36 mg) were dissolved in 1.0 L deionized water to prepare a Ni-CDs stock solution. K2CrO4 aqueous solution was continuously injected into a cuvette (12 mm × 12 mm × 44 mm) containing 3.5 mL Ni-CDs stock solution, and the cuvette was oscillated for 1 min to evenly disperse Cr(VI). Fluorescence spectra were collected for each injection of Cr(VI) aqueous solution until the fluorescence was quenched by more than two thirds of the original intensity.

Similarly, 1.0%-Ni-CDs-M was tailored to be a rectangle (12 mm × 40 mm) and its upper and lower ends were fixed on a thin sheet of glass. The thin sheet of glass was placed diagonally in the cuvette with the PA layer facing the direction of incident light. Subsequently, deionized water was poured into the cuvette, and K2CrO4 aqueous solution was constantly injected, and the cuvette was vibrated for 2 min to guarantee the sufficient interaction between Cr(VI) and Ni-CDs in the NF membrane. Fluorescence spectra of Cr(VI) titrating 0.75%-Ni-CDs-M were therefore collected. The operation process for real-time monitoring of Cr(VI) in wastewater is similar to that of fluorescence spectra of Cr(VI) titrating 0.75%-Ni-CDs-M, but K2CrO4 aqueous solution was displaced by Cr(VI)-containing wastewater only.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication, characterization, optical properties, and fluorescence response to Cr(VI) of Ni-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Metal ion-doped CDs usually exhibit good nanozyme activities due to metal-mediated photophysicochemical reactions [54]. Moreover, the surface charge of CDs is dominated by a precursor with a dominant charge. PIP, as an IP reaction reagent, was therefore selected as a precursor to coordinate with Ni2+ for the synthesis of Ni-CDs via a co-hydrothermal reaction with multi-negatively charged Na2EDTA (Scheme 1 A). Ni-CDs were expected to integrate into the PA layer of the NF membrane by IP for the separation of polyvalent anions with high cytotoxicity from the effluent via the Donnan effect. The as-prepared Ni-CDs were then characterized to determine their morphology, structural characteristics, and charge performance (Apparatus and Characterizations are presented in Supporting Information (SI)). The TEM image reveals that the mean diameter of the Ni-CDs is 3.6 nm (Fig. S1). The broad diffraction peak at 22.7° and the shoulder peak at 41.2° in the XRD pattern imply that the Ni-CDs are essentially amorphous (Fig. S2). The zeta-potential (−14.9 eV) indicates that the Ni-CDs are highly negatively charged (Fig. S3). Both FTIR and locally amplified FTIR spectra (Fig. S4), and XPS spectra of the Ni-CDs (Fig. S5) verify that Ni2+ was successfully doped into the CDs through a coordination interaction and a large number of carboxylic acid but a small quantity of imine covered the surface of Ni-CDs. Ni-CDs can synchronously emit fluorescence and RTP. The short-lived fluorescence of Ni-CDs aqueous solution (1.36 ns) and long-lived RTP of Ni-CDs powder (39.21 μs) were determined (Figs. S6–S7).

The RTP emission performance of the Ni-CDs suggests that they can be used as photosensitizers for biofouling resistance [55–57]. The anti-bacterial potential of the Ni-CDs was surveyed here using E. coli as a model strain (Antibacterial Properties Test in SI). The Ni-CDs aqueous solutions (10 mg/L) were co-cultured with E. coli under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation and darkness, respectively. After 1 h, the samples were plated for colony-counting. As expected, almost no bacterial colonies were visible for the sample under light irradiation (Fig. 1 A), but numerous bacterial colonies were visible all over the plate for the sample under darkness (Fig. 1B). The E. coli concentration was 1.24 × 104 CFU/mL for the sample without light illumination as determined by performing quantitative analysis. The bacterial inhibition rate was measured to be 98.4 % for the sample under light irradiation. Similarly, a photograph of the Ni-CDs in the middle of a Petri dish evenly coated with E. coli under light irradiation shows the same result as those of the biofouling resistant test (Fig. S8). Similar to E. coli, B. subtilis as the model bacterium gave the same results (Fig. 1A’) (Fig. 1B’). These results distinctly indicate that the Ni-CDs have a crucial photodynamic anti-bacterial nature.

Given that carboxylic acid and active nitrogen (pyridinic N, metal-coordinated N) groups similar to amino acids covered the surface of Ni-CDs, the pH-dependent emission of the Ni-CDs aqueous solution was explored to assess their anti-acidic functionality [58]. Fig. 1C displays that the fluorescence intensity gradually enhanced with pH value from pH 3.0 to 4.0, then remained stable from pH 4.0 to 8.5, and finally sharply increased (pH > 8.5) (inset in Fig. 1C), revealing that Ni-CDs are pH-sensitive nanomaterials. Moreover, the effects of the external addition of a small amount of NaOH and dilution with water on the pH of the Ni-CDs aqueous solution were studied, and an HCl solution was utilized as a control. In contrast to the rapid increase in the pH of NaOH solution titrating HCl solution, the reposeful increase in the pH of NaOH solution titrating Ni-CDs aqueous solution was discovered (Fig. S9A). Similarly, the changes in the pH of these two kinds of solutions upon dilution exhibited the same tendency as those of NaOH titrating (Fig. S9B). These results indicate that Ni-CDs can be served as an electrolyte to resist the impact of external stimuli, such as strong base addition or dilution, on the pH of the system through the generation of two types of reversible forms (–NH2 +– — –NH– and —COOH—COO-—) [58], ensuring that the pH of the system fluctuates within a safe range.

Fluorescence response of the Ni-CDs to Cr(VI) was involved here. As described in (Fig. 1D), after dosing Cr(VI) into the Ni-CDs aqueous solution (36 mg/L), the fluorescence was gradually quenched, and the spectral redshift occurred. The plots of log (I0/I) (where I0 and I are the original and instant fluorescence intensities at 428 nm, respectively) vs Cr(VI) concentration in the range of 0.1–100.0 ppm exhibited a linear relationship as follows:
(3) Y = 0.0090C–0.0018, R2 = 0.9966 (LOD: 0.076 ppm)
where Y and C are log (I0/I) and the Cr(VI) concentration in ppm, respectively. LOD is the limit of detection. A significant difference in the brightness of the photographs before and after the addition of 50 ppm of Cr(VI) under a 365 nm UV lamp was observed, indicating that the Ni-CDs probe has a high-sensitivity response to Cr(VI) (insets in Fig. 1D). The interferences from possible co-existing anions and cations in real-world samples were analyzed. First, after the addition of 100 ppm of Cr(VI) to the Ni-CDs aqueous solution, I0/I was found to be > 8.0. However, the addition of 1.0 mM of common anions, including HPO4 2−, H2PO4 −, F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, SO4 2−, NO3 −, CO3 2−, and ClO4 −, resulted in no significant changes in fluorescence intensity. Namely, I0/I is approximately equal to 1.0 (Fig. 1E). Next, I0/I of the Ni-CDs aqueous solution after the addition of 50 ppm of Cr(VI) was found to be the same as those after the addition of an equal amount of Cr(VI) with 1.0 mM of other cations, such as NH4 +, K+, Na+, Li+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Pb2+, and Cd2+ (Fig. 1F). These results indicate that Cr(VI) can be detected by the Ni-CDs fluorescence probe without interference from the abovementioned routine anions and cations. We found that Cu2+ and Fe3+ influenced on the determination of Cr(VI). Most fortunately, Cu2+ and Fe3+ were capable of efficient shielding by thiourea and phosphoric acid, respectively (Fig. S10) [59]. Therefore, the developed Ni-CDs-based chemosensor is expected to enable the facile and selective detection of Cr(VI).

PIP, a reactant for IP reaction, was used to synthesize Ni-CDs, which was naturally expected to integrate into the PA layer of the NF membranes. In addition, the Ni-CDs exhibit an excellent photodynamic anti-bacteria, good hydrophilicity, and a selective response to Cr(VI). We expected that the membranes to be prepared will inherit these significant merits for highly efficient removal and real-time monitoring of Cr(VI) in wastewater. In this study, various amounts of Ni-CDs (x%, x = 0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, and 1.0) and PIP were interfacially polymerized with TMC onto a PES substrate to fabricate NF membranes (Scheme 1B) (Preparation of PES Substrate Membrane in SI). The resulting Ni-CDs-Ms were characterized by fluorescence, ATR-FTIR, contact angle, AFM, SEM and pore size evaluation. As exhibited in (Fig. 2 A), the fluorescence intensity of Ni-CDs-Ms gradually boosted with the increase in Ni-CDs content in the membranes. The fluorescence of 0%-Ni-CDs-M possibly originated from the aromatic groups in the membrane, and that of other Ni-CDs-Ms derived from the fluorescence addition of the aromatic groups and Ni-CDs. The ATR-FTIR spectra uncover that the peaks at 1524, 1620, and 1668 cm−1 could be assigned to the C–N stretching, N–H bending vibrations, and CO absorption of the amides, respectively (Fig. 2B) [55–58] (Fig. 2C). shows that the contact angles of the Ni-CDs-Ms constantly decreased with increasing Ni-CDs content in membranes, which can be assigned to the excellent wettability of the Ni-CDs (Fig. S11A). Moreover, the surface roughness of the Ni-CDs-Ms took on the same change as the contact angle measurement except for the slight increase of the 1.0%-Ni-CDs-M (Fig. S11B), uncovering that when the Ni-CDs were conformally deposited on the substrate membrane, and chemical bonding at the bottom of the membrane surface was preferential (Fig. S11C), resulting in the ceaseless improvement in surface smoothness. Accordingly, the conjugation of Ni-CDs optimized the surface topography of the NF membranes.

The ability of the Ni-CDs to finely regulate the pore size of the PA layer was explored by the PEG permeation method (Pore size evaluation of Ni-CDs-Ms in SI) [58]. As illustrated in (Fig. 2D), the active layer of 0%-Ni-CDs-M displayed a maximum pore size of 1.34 nm. Smaller pore sizes were achieved with further integration of the Ni-CDs: 10.7 nm for 0.25%-Ni-CDs-M, 1.04 nm for 0.5%-Ni-CDs-M, 1.03 nm for 0.75%-Ni-CDs-M, 1.01 nm for 1.0%-Ni-CDs-M. Obviously, the increase in Ni-CDs content in IP led to the smaller pore size of the PA layer. The above results not only manifest that the Ni-CDs were successfully coupled in the PA layer, but also significantly improved the physicochemical properties of the NF membranes, which contributed to the enhanced water flux and salt rejection (vide post), and guided the direction for accurately regulating the microstructure of PA membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Separation performance tests of Ni-CDs-Ms and effects of pH and operation time
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the separation performance of the as-prepared Ni-CDs-Ms, various inorganic salts with a same concentration (1000 ppm), such as MgSO4, Na2SO4, MgCl2, NaCl, Na2HPO4, and NaH2PO4, as feed solutions and operation pressure of 3 bar were conducted. As described in (Fig. 3 A), for every inorganic salt, the water flux first increased with increasing Ni-CDs content from 0 % to 0.75 %, and then reduced with the increase in the Ni-CDs content to 1.0 %. For all inorganic salt solutions, 0.75%-Ni-CDs-M exhibited the maximum water flux. The maximum water fluxes in the case of NaCl, Na2HPO4, and NaH2PO4 feed solutions were approximately equal, and slightly greater than those in the case of MgSO4, Na2SO4, and MgCl2 feed solutions, which also showed approximately equal maximum water fluxes. The boosted water flux can be attributed to the fact that the integration of Ni-CDs improved the wettability of the NF membranes. A high load of the Ni-CDs (1.0 %) thickened the PA layer and narrowed the pore size, resulting in the reduction in water flux (Fig. 2D) [60,61]. Since the conjugation of negatively charged Ni-CDs gave rise to a much more negative charge of membrane surface, for all inorganic salt solutions, the salt rejection of the Ni-CDs-Ms sharply elevated with the increase in Ni-CDs content to 0.25 %, then gradually lowered with the Ni-CDs content range from 0.25 % to 0.75 %, suddenly intensified in the end due to the same reason as the reduction in water fluxes at the maximum Ni-CDs content (Fig. 3B). Most significantly, the rejection of bivalent anions (SO4 2−, and HPO4 2−) Ni-CDs-Ms was high but that of monovalent anions (Cl−, and H2PO4 −) was low, suggesting that Ni-CDs-Ms can be used for hypervalent anion separation.

The effects of the operation time and pH on the separation performance were investigated. The time-dependent water flux and salt rejection of the Ni-CDs-Ms remained almost unchanged when Na2HPO4 feed solution was used (Fig. 3C–D), indicating the excellent operation stability of the Ni-CDs-Ms. The pH-dependent separation performance was also considered using Na2SO4 as a feed solution, and pH values 4.0, 7.0, and 10.0 were selected. As shown in (Fig. 3E–F), except for the water flux of 0%-Ni-CDs-M at pH 4.0, every piece of Ni-CDs-Ms displayed ceaseless increases in water flux and salt rejection with the increase in pH value. In addition, at the same pH and with the unceasing load of the Ni-CDs, the water flux and salt rejection of the Ni-CDs-Ms gave the same changes as those for every inorganic salt (Fig. 3A–B). These results can be attributed to the fact that the increased pH led to a much more negative zeta-potential of the membrane surface, resulting in a stronger Donan effect for divalent anions (vide post).

E. coli and B. subtilis used as model strains to survey whether the 0.25–1.0%-Ni-CDs-Ms naturally inherited the anti-bacterial/acidic functionalities of the Ni-CDs (Scheme 1C). Four sets of identical samples for E. coli and S. aureus, respectively, were prepared co-culturing under light irradiation and darkness. As expected, bacterial colonies across all the plates were observed in the dark group (data not shown). Conversely, obviously fewer bacterial colonies in the corresponding light irradiation group than those in the dark group were discovered, demonstrating the significant nanozyme activity of the 0.25–1.0%-Ni-CDs-Ms. Notably, even integration of a small number of Ni-CDs (0.25 %) in the membrane caused a geometric increase in anti-bacterial functionality (Fig. S12-top and bottom). Especially with a further increase in Ni-CDs content in the membrane from 0.5 % to 1.0 %, almost no bacterial colonies were visible on the corresponding plates, demonstrating that the natural inheritance of the enzyme activity of Ni-CDs in the NF membrane. In addition, BSA as the model to test the bio-fouling resistance of the membrane was also conducted (BSA anti-fouling text in SI), and the result shows that the Ni-CDs-Ms had an excellent anti-fouling performance (Fig. S13). In all, the anti-bacterial ratio over 98.4 % (Table S3) and flux recover ratio (FRR) equal to 96.9 % (Table S4) verified that the Ni-CDs-Ms have excellent biofouling resistances.

The effects of the acidification time on the water flux and salt rejection using Na2SO4 as a feed solution were next studied (Scheme 1D). As illustrated in Fig. S14A, the water fluxes of 0%-Ni-CDs-M and 0.25%-Ni-CDs-M slowly and promptly enhanced, respectively, when these two pieces of membranes were subjected to acidification treatment for more than 4 h, and however, the water fluxes of the other Ni-CDs-Ms remained relatively stable, which can be resulted from the acidolysis of the PA layer [58]. Likewise, both the decrease to different extent in salt rejection of the 0–0.25%-Ni-CDs-Ms, and the almost invariableness in that of the other Ni-CDs-Ms could be reasonably explained (Fig. S14B). The above results verify that the integration of Ni-CDs in the PA layer endowed NF membranes with excellent biofouling/acidic resistance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Highly efficient rejection and real-time monitoring of Cr(VI) in wastewater
ce:section.ce:para:
Given the outstanding separation performance of 0.25–1.0%- Ni-CDs-Ms for divalent anions, these NF membranes are expected to be applied for the highly efficient rejection and real-time monitoring of Cr(VI) in wastewater (Scheme 1E). (Fig. 4 A) shows the distribution coefficient of chromic acid. When pH is near 8.5, CrO4 2− is the dominant form of Cr(VI). Therefore, the pH of the system was set to 8.5 in the experiments on the water flux, rejection and sensing of Cr(VI). Coincidentally, the fluorescence emission of the Ni-CDs located at the stable platform at this pH (inset in Fig. 1C). The water flux and Cr(VI) rejection of the Ni-CDs-Ms exhibited the same changes as those of any of the other inorganic salts (Fig. 4B). Considering the high water flux and acceptable Cr(VI) rejection greater than 95 %, 0.75%-Ni-CDs-M was selected for the removal and monitoring of Cr(VI) in this study. The fluorescence spectra of Cr(VI) titrating 0.75%-Ni-CDs-M were conducted. After dosing Cr(VI) into the 0.75%-Ni-CDs-M aqueous solution, the left bulge of spectra owing to Rayleigh scattering [62], and the continually quenched fluorescence and gradually redshifted spectra were observed (Fig. 4C). The plots of log (I0/I) vs Cr(VI) concentration in the range of 0.08–11.6 ppm exhibited a linear relationship as below:
(4) Y = 0.05443C–0.0094, R2 = 0.9987 (LOD: 0.072 ppm)
where Y and C are the same as those in Eq. (3) (inset in Fig. 4C). Notably, the membrane-based chemosensor exhibited a much greater sensitivity than Ni-CDs-based sensor (Fig. 1D), and a nearly 10-fold increase in sensitivity was achieved. Compared with the reported works, the Cr(VI) detection based on our Ni-CDs-M chemosensor exhibits a relatively broader linear range and a much lower limit of detection (Table S5). The accuracy and reliability were evaluated under real conditions, the present chemosensor was conducted to detect Cr(VI) in environmental water samples. The samples via a simple pretreatment and adjustment of pH to ∼8.5 were analyzed by a standard-addition method by injecting certain amounts of Cr(VI) into the real-world water samples. The measured results well coincided with the spiked Cr(VI) concentrations, and the quantitative recovery ratios from 96.9 % to 104.6 % were obtained (Table S6). Accordingly, a membrane-based sensor has been established for real-time warning of abnormal changes in Cr(VI) level in wastewater, and providing the technical support for the prevention and handling of the sudden Cr(VI) incidents in environment.

The effects of the pH, Cr(VI) concentration, acidification time, and operational pressure and time on the separation performance of 0.75%-Ni-CDs-M were evaluated. Likewise, pH values of 4.0, 7.0, and 10.0 were selected. Both the water flux and Cr(VI) rejection increased in an ascending order with the rise in pH value (Fig. 4D). Conversely, both the water flux and Cr(VI) rejection decreased with the integral multiple increase in Cr(VI) concentration (Fig. 4E). As above mentioned high anti-acidic functionality of the 0.5–1.0%-Ni-CDs-Ms using Na2SO4 as a feed solution (Figs. S14A–B), the water flux and Cr(VI) rejection of the 0.75%-Ni-CDs-M fluctuated within normal limits with the extension in acidification time using chromate as a feed solution (Fig. 4F), therefore, 0.75%-Ni-CDs-M was considered suitable for practical applications. In addition, as the pressure increased, the high-level Cr(VI) rejection remained nearly unchanged, and the water flux gradually boosted (Fig. S15). No obvious effects of the operation time on water flux and Cr(VI) rejection were revealed (Fig. S16). Therefore, a novel 0.75%-Ni-CDs-M has been developed for highly efficient rejection and real-time monitoring of Cr(VI) in wastewater. The separation and real-time Cr(VI)monitoring performances of Ni-CDs-Ms were superior to those of other approaches, making the former more suitable for commercial applications. Moreover, the integration of cheap and easy-to-prepare Ni-CDs made the NF membrane exhibit anti-bacterial/-acidic performances, contributing to the sustainable and economical operation of the NF membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanisms of anti-bacteria/acid, and rejection and monitoring of Cr(VI) of Ni-CDs-Ms
ce:section.ce:para: The explorations on the mechanisms of anti-bacteria/acid, and rejection and sensing of Cr(VI) of Ni-CDs-Ms are of great importance. The EPR spectra of the solutions containing Ni-CDs and TMPO before and after UV light irradiation for 1 min were measured. As depicted in (Fig. 5 A), a triplet peak with an equal intensity was found in the EPR curve after light irradiation for 1 min, which is the characteristic peak of 1O2, indicating that the Ni-CDs can be served as photosensitizers, and responding to the RTP emission of the Ni-CDs. Moreover, a TMB-based colorimetric analysis was conducted. After 1 min of light irradiation, the colorless TMB aqueous solution remained unchanged, but the mixed solution containing TMB and Ni-CDs changed from colorless to blue. Once tryptophan, a 1O2 scavenger, was added to the above mixture, the change in color was not observed (Fig. 5B), further verifying the photodynamic anti-bacterial activity of the Ni-CDs (Scheme1C) [55–57]. As described above, the surface of the Ni-CDs bears carboxylic acid and imine groups, which have acidic/basic activities and can serve as an electrolyte. With the rise in pH (e.g. pH 8.5), on the one hand, carboxylic acid anions on the surface of Ni-CDs in Ni-CDs-Ms increase, and on the other hand, the positively charged –NH2 +— group at low pH changed to be neutral —NH— group (Scheme 1D), resulting in a much more negative Zeta potential (vide post). Most interestingly, whether the pH of the Ni-CDs aqueous solution was adjusted from acid to base or reversely, the plots of fluorescence intensity vs pH were almost overlapped (Fig. 5C), demonstrating that the reversible anti-acidic functionality of the Ni-CDs and 0.25–1.0%-Ni-CDs-Ms (Scheme1D).

The responsive mechanism of the Ni-CDs to Cr(VI) was simply discussed here. The normalized UV–vis spectrum of Cr(VI), and the excitation and emission spectra of Ni-CDs are presented in (Fig. 5D), the absorption peak at 358 nm of Cr(VI) mostly overlaps with the excitation wavelength of the Ni-CDs, and the shoulder absorption from 412 to 525 nm partially overlaps with the emission wavelength of the Ni-CDs. Therefore, the mechanism responsive to Cr(VI) can be well explained by an inner filtration effect (IFE) [63]. Notably, the IFE-based fluorescence approach is more flexible and straightforward than traditional methods: it does not have the distance limit between the energy donor and receptor [64], and it is particularly suitable for the developed membrane-based sensors. We also assayed the reason for the high rejection of divalent anions by the 0.25–1.0%-Ni-CDs-Ms through zeta-potential measurement. The hydrated ionic radius of Cr(VI) (1.24 Å) and 5.05 Å of the smallest pore radius of our Ni-CDs-Ms indicate that size sieving effect cannot play a key role in the separation of monovalent and divalent anions. A certain salt rejection exhibited by the 0%-Ni-CDs-M with a low negative charge indicated that the anion separation occurred mainly because of the size sieving (Fig. 3B). A large amount of carboxylic acid covering the surface of the Ni-CDs caused a more negative zeta-potential with the increase in pH (Fig. 5E). Similarly, a higher pH created a more negative zeta-potential of the membrane surface (Fig. 5F), resulting in the much better separation performances of 0.25–1.0%-Ni-CDs-Ms, and showing that the Donnan effect was the dominant factor. In short, the synergy of size sieving and the Donnan effect achieved anion separation, and the dominance of these factors varied under different conditions.

In summary, Ni-CDs were fabricated by a hydrothermal method using Ni2+, PIP, and Na2EDTA as precursors, which showed a highly negative charge, photosensitivity and acid-resistance. We integrated the Ni-CDs into the PA layer to prepare Ni-CDs-Ms by IP, and regulation of the microstructure of the NF membrane and improvement of its physicochemical properties, such as hydrophilicity and roughness of the membrane surface, were achieved, resulting in a much better separation performance for divalent anions. In particular, the NF membrane inherited the biofouling/acid resistance from the Ni-CDs, helping to reduce the operation cost and prolong the operation lifetime of the NF membrane. Notably, both the Ni-CDs and 0.25–1.0%-Ni-CDs-Ms can selectively respond to Cr(VI) in wastewater. The 0.75%-Ni-CDs-M was singled out for ultra-high sensitivity real-time monitoring of Cr(VI) in wastewater and its high-efficiency Cr(VI) removal. Further studies elucidated the possible mechanisms of anti-bacteria/acid, and rejection and sensing of Cr(VI) of the Ni-CDs-Ms. This protocol contributed to the sustainable and economical operation of the NF membrane for the elimination of divalent anions with high cytotoxicity.

Weili Kong: Methodology, Conceptualization. Xia Chen: Validation, Funding acquisition. Jun Zhang: Data curation, Conceptualization. Weihua Li: Writing – review & editing, Investigation.
